locked_facts: Dipole moment p = q × 2a, directed from negative to positive charge
Axial field (on the line of dipole): E_axial = 2kp/r³ for r >> a
Equatorial field (perpendicular bisector): E_equatorial = kp/r³ for r >> a
Axial field is twice the equatorial field at the same distance
Equatorial field points opposite to dipole moment direction
Torque on dipole in uniform field: τ = p × E = pE sinθ
Potential energy of dipole in field: U = -p·E = -pE cosθ
Net force on dipole in uniform field is zero — only torque acts
truth_statements: The electric field of a dipole falls off as 1/r³ at large distances, faster than a point charge field which falls as 1/r²
The dipole moment is defined as the product of charge magnitude and separation, directed from negative to positive
In a uniform electric field a dipole experiences a torque that tends to align it with the field but no net translational force
The axial field is exactly twice the equatorial field at the same large distance from the dipole
At the equatorial point the field direction is antiparallel to the dipole moment

aha_moment.what_to_show: Split screen showing field vectors at an axial point and an equatorial point at equal distance r — the axial arrow is exactly twice as long as the equatorial arrow, and the equatorial arrow points opposite to p
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Side-by-side comparison of axial (2kp/r³) and equatorial (kp/r³) field magnitudes with arrows
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students assume the dipole field is symmetric in all directions — seeing the 2:1 ratio and direction reversal makes the anisotropy undeniable

simulation_states.label: Dipole at rest
simulation_states.description: Two charges +q and -q separated by 2a. The dipole moment vector p is shown from -q to +q. No field lines yet.
simulation_states.emphasis: Establish p direction — negative to positive

simulation_states.label: Field lines appear
simulation_states.description: Electric field lines emerge from +q and terminate on -q. The characteristic dipole pattern is visible. Field vectors are drawn at one axial and one equatorial point.
simulation_states.emphasis: Field lines curve from + to - and the pattern is NOT symmetric around the midpoint

simulation_states.label: Axial vs equatorial comparison
simulation_states.description: At equal distance r from the centre: axial field arrow is drawn twice as long as equatorial field arrow. Equatorial arrow points opposite to p. Numerical values confirm E_axial = 2 × E_equatorial.
simulation_states.emphasis: The 2:1 ratio and the direction reversal at the equatorial point are the core insight

simulation_states.label: Dipole in uniform field
simulation_states.description: Uniform external E field applied. Dipole experiences torque τ = pE sinθ. Student rotates dipole with slider — torque arrow changes. At θ=0 stable equilibrium, at θ=180° unstable.
simulation_states.emphasis: Torque tries to align p with E — zero net force in uniform field

common_misconceptions.belief: The dipole field is the same magnitude in all directions at a given distance
common_misconceptions.correction: The axial field is exactly twice the equatorial field at the same distance (E_axial = 2kp/r³ vs E_equatorial = kp/r³)
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show two field arrows at same distance r — axial one twice as long

common_misconceptions.belief: The dipole moment points from positive to negative charge
common_misconceptions.correction: By convention the dipole moment p points from the negative charge to the positive charge
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Clearly label p arrow from -q to +q with arrow direction
